IN THE HIGH COURT OF LESOTHO
(Commercial Court Division)
HELD AT MASERU                                                 CIV/T/0345/2021
In the matter between:
CHINIA CHARLES                                                               APPLICANT
And
THABO MANYALA                                                              1
ST
CLAIMANT
THE REGISTRAR                                                                 2
ND
CLAIMANT
THE ATTORNEY GENERAL                                               3
RD
CLAIMANT
‘MABATAUNG KHAJOANE                                                4
TH
CLAIMANT
Neutral Citation: Chinia Charles v. Thabo Manyala & 3 Others
[2026] LSHC 53
COM (5
th
March 2026)
JUDGMENT
CORAM:                       MATHABA J
HEARD ON:                  4
th
December 2025
DELIVERED ON:           5
th
March 2026
SUMMARY:
Execution – Interpleader proceedings – A motor vehicle attached and removed from judgement debtor in execution of a writ of execution – Judgment debtor in the process of registering the vehicle in Lesotho at the time of attachment – Applicant, a South African citizen, claiming the motor vehicle as his and producing certificate of registration in his names – Applicant contending that he sold the motor vehicle to another Lesotho resident who still has outstanding balance to pay – Judgment creditor contending that the applicant is colluding with the judgment debtor – Possession of a movable creating presumption of ownership – Onus of proof – Sufficiency of evidence – The circumstances of the case inviting suspicion – Applicant required to produce clear and satisfactory proof of ownership – Applicant failing to adduce evidence available to him to corroborate his version that the vehicle was not sold to the judgment debtor and that he retained ownership thereof – The application dismissed with costs.
ANNOTATIONS:
STATUTES
High Court Civil Litigation Rules Legal Notice N0. 65 of 2024
CASES
Lesotho
Reitumetsi Masoone v. Pinki ‘Mareitumetsi Masooane and 3 Others CIV/APN/14/2021)
[2021] LSHC 38
(22 April 2021)
South- Africa
Embrahim v Deputy Sheriff Durban and Another 1961 (4) SA 267 (D)
Eriksen Motors (Welkom) Ltd v. Protea Motors, Warrenton 1973 (3) SA 685 AD
Gleneagles Farm Dairy v Schoombee 1949 (1) SA 830 (A)
K & D Motors v Wessels 1949 (1) SA 1 (A)
Marks & Lamb Classic Cars CC v. Mbulelo Kona (80288-17)
[2019] ZAGPPHC 3
(16 January 2019)
Introduction
[1]     The issue in these “Interpleader proceedings” is whether the applicant has proved his ownership of a motor vehicle attached by the Deputy Sheriff subsequent to a writ of execution issued by the fourth claimant.
The parties
[2]     The applicant is a businessman residing in Durban in the Republic of South Africa. He claims ownership and seeks the release of a vehicle whose particulars appear fully below.
[3]     The first claimant is Thabo Manyala, adult male resident of Mokhotlong. He is execution debtor from whose possession the vehicle was removed pursuant to a writ of execution. He subsequently instituted an application claiming the release of the vehicle.
[4]     The second claimant is the Registrar of the High Court in whose possession the vehicle is after it was attached and removed from the first claimant.
[5]     The third claimant is the Attorney General cited in these proceedings in his capacity as the representative of the second claimant in civil suits.
[6]     The fourth claimant is ‘Mabataung Khajoane adult female resident of Mokhotlong. She is the judgment creditor on whose account the writ of execution was issued having successfully sued the first claimant.
Background facts
[7]     On 15 February 2022 the fourth claimant obtained a default judgment against the execution debtor, for
inter alia
payment of an amount of M29,000.00 together with interest and costs of suit. The fourth claimant’s action against the execution debtor was based on a breach of contract concluded between the fourth claimant and the execution debtor for the sale and purchase of a piece of land located at Checha, in the district of Mokhotlong.
[8]     Pursuant to the default judgment granted in favour of the fourth claimant, a writ of execution was issued by the Registrar of the High Court on 2 June 2022 in favour of the fourth claimant. The Deputy-Sheriff proceeded to execute the writ and in November 2023 laid under judicial attachment a vehicle of the following description:
8.1     Volkswagen Sprinter Engine Number: CKT038976, Chassis Number: WV1ZZZ2EZD6011548.
[9]     The first claimant reacted with application for rescission of an order granted against him and for the release of the vehicle into his possession. The basis of a claim for the release was that the vehicle belongs to a company, and that at the time it was attached and removed, the claimant was in the process of registering it for the company. The application is opposed by the fourth claimant.
[10]   On 14 December 2023 and by consent of the parties, an order for stay of the sale in execution of the vehicle was granted pending finalisation of the application. The application is still pending before this Court.
[11]   On 4 February 2025 the applicant instituted “interpleader proceedings” claiming ownership of the vehicle. The applicant states that he sold the vehicle to one Lelingoana Lelingoana who made part payment; that Lelingoana was going to lease out the vehicle at Polihali Dam construction and pay monthly instalments with proceeds generated therefrom; and that Lelingoana has been unable to pay since the vehicle was attached. Certificate of registration for the vehicle in the names of the applicant is annexed to the application.
[12]   The fourth claimant was not initially joined in the “interpleader proceedings”. Her counsel fortuitously learned of the “interpleader proceedings” on 24 February 2025 when he came to court to paginate the court file. In response, the fourth claimant sought leave to join and same was granted on 24 February 2025 by consent of the parties. She has filed an answering affidavit opposing the claim by the applicant.
[13]   The fourth claimant dispute the applicant’s claim and contends that inasmuch as the certificate of registration is still in the applicant’s names, he transferred his ownership in the vehicle and that he is colluding with the first claimant who lacks prospects of success in the rescission application. Further that at the time the vehicle was attached and removed, the first claimant was in the process of registering it in Lesotho.
[14]   The contest in this matter is between the applicant and the fourth claimant as other parties have not shown interest in the matter including the first claimant despite having claimed the release of the vehicle immediately following its attachment. These parties would seemingly abide by the order of the Court.
Applicable law
[15]   Before dealing with the respective claims, a short exposition of the applicable law. Interpleader proceedings are governed by rule 169 of the High Court Civil Litigation Rules 2024.
[1]
Given the scheme of rule 169, interpleader proceedings in the context of attachment in execution have to be initiated by the Sheriff or the Deputy Sheriff as the applicant, not a party with ownership or beneficial interest in the property.
[16]   The rationale behind interpleader proceedings is that the Sheriff or Deputy Sheriff has power to attach movable and immovable property belonging to a judgment debtor. And that since it is possible for property belonging to a third party to be attached if it is found in the possession of the judgment debtor, interpleader proceedings give the third party an opportunity to claim his/her goods. Thus, in issuing interpleader proceedings, the applicant must call upon the claimants to deliver particulars of their claim with all documentary evidence and exhibits. This enables the court to adjudicate on the claims after such evidence as it may deem just.
[17]   Where movable goods attached are in possession of a judgment debtor and a third party claims those goods as his/hers, that third party is burdened with the onus to prove his/her claim to the goods. This is firstly because the third party is the claimant and secondly, because possession of a movable creates the presumption of ownership.
[2]
The presumption “
can be swept away and upset by evidence
” hence it must be considered in view of the circumstances of each particular case.
[3]
[18]   As regards ownership of a vehicle, registration or certificate of registration is a
prima facie
proof of ownership of the motor vehicle in favour of the person in whose names the vehicle is registered – though a rebuttable presumption.
[4]
Even in the context of South Africa’s National Road Traffic
Act No. 93 of 1996
, the possession of registration papers is just
prima facie
proof of ownership and not conclusive proof of ownership.
[5]
I am referring to this Act because the certificate of registration the applicant produced was issued in South Africa.
[19]   It is trite that the contract of sale does not pass ownership to the purchaser. For ownership to take place the parties must intend for the ownership to pass and delivery must be effected. In
Eriksen Motors (Welkom) Ltd v. Protea Motors, Warrenton
[6]
Holmes JA stated that the general rule is that –
“(a) In a sale for cash ownership does not pass until the price is
paid, even if delivery has meantime being given;
(b) In a sale on credit, ownership passes on delivery.
This, however, is not an irrefrangible principle of law. It is basically a question of fact in each case.  It depends whether the totality of the circumstances shows, by inference or otherwise, that the parties intended ownership to pass or not to pass, as the case might be. Thus, in Commissioner of Customs & Excise v. Randles, Brothers & Hudson, Ltd., 1941 A.D. 369 at p. 398, WATERMEYER, J.A., said this-
“Ownership of movable property does not in our law pass by the making of a contract. It passes when delivery of possession is given accompanied by an intention on the part of the transferor to transfer ownership and on the part of the transferee to receive it. If it is delivered in pursuance of a contract of sale, the ownership may pass at the time of delivery or it may not... The form of the contract between the parties... has considerable value because, if the parties put their contract in the form of a sale on credit, a strong inference can be drawn that they intended a delivery in pursuance of that contract to transfer ownership... Again, whether or not an intention to transfer ownership by delivery exists is a question of fact, not of law.  If the parties desire to transfer ownership and contemplate that ownership will pass as a result of delivery, then they in fact have the necessary intention and the ownership passes by delivery.”
Applying the law to the facts
[20]   I must first state the obvious. These are not interpleader proceedings proper in that they have not been issued out by the Sheriff or the Deputy Sheriff as provided for under rule 169. Neither have they been instituted by the person who is in possession of the vehicle and does not have a beneficial interest in it. Nor have claimants been invited to submit their particulars of claim.
[21]   The applicant recognises in his founding papers that interpleader proceedings have to be instituted by the Sheriff or the Deputy Sheriff. He contends that his counsel approached the Registrar (Sheriff) in September 2024 asking her to file interpleader proceedings but that she did not until he decided to take matters into his own hands and instituted the instant application.
[22]   Be that as it may, the parties were happy to proceed with the application as it is. Additionally, on 30 September 2025 both Mr Masoeu for the applicant and Mr Motsoehli for the fourth claimant appeared before me virtually and stated that the parties do not wish to call any further evidence thus the matter must be decided on papers as they stand. Despite my observation concerning non-compliance with rule 169, and given the parties attitude, I sought inspiration from the following remarks from the Constitutional Court of South Africa in
PFE International Inc. (BVI) and Others v. Industrial Development Corporation of South Africa Ltd
[7]
and resolved to entertain the merits of the matter in the interest of justice:
“[30] Since the rules are the made for the courts to facilitate the adjudication of cases, the superior courts enjoy the power to regulate their processes, taking into account the interests of justice.  It is this power that makes every superior court the master of its own process.  It enables a superior to lay down a process to be followed in particular cases, even if that process deviates from what its rules prescribe. Consistent with that power, this court may in the interests of justice depart from its own rules.
[31] .... In some cases a mechanical application of a particular rule may lead to an injustice… It is therefore necessary for courts to have power to adjust the application of rules to avoid injustices.  Moreover, the court rules are tailored to facilitate introduction and management of cases under the courts’ supervision.”
(Footnotes omitted).
[23]   As it has already been indicated, in support of ownership of the vehicle, the certificate of registration of the vehicle in the names of the applicant has been attached to the application. Nevertheless, it bears repeating that the certificate is not conclusive proof of ownership. The vehicle was found in possession of judgment debtor who was in the process of registering it in Lesotho. It is therefore for the applicant to displace the presumption that the vehicle belongs to the judgment debtor. The applicant says the following in explanation how the vehicle came to Lesotho:
“
5.4   In contrast, I aver that the vehicle belongs to me and I am selling same to one
Lelingoana Lelingoana
, who has not yet completed the payment for the vehicle. This is the reason why the ownership of the vehicle has not yet been transferred to him as can be seen on the registration certificate.  The buyer, who I have had other business agreements with, had informed me that the vehicle was purchased with the intention of being hired out in
Mokhotlong
where
Polihali
dam construction project is undertaken.  Upon it being hired, the balance owing will then be paid from the money the vehicle is making monthly by him.  Since the attachment of the vehicle,
Lelingoana
has been unable to pay the remaining balance, which is why I am now claiming the vehicle.
5.5     According to information that I have, the vehicle was attached while still in the possession of the 1st Claimant.  The 1st Claimant had been requested by
Lelingoana
to find work for the vehicle at
Polihali,
as the 1st Claimant resides in
Mokhotlong
and residents there are given preference for employment opportunities over people from other regions of the country at the
Polihali
dam construction.”
[24]   Conversely, the fourth claimant contends that the applicant is no longer the owner of the vehicle. According to her, the applicant is colluding with the first claimant, the judgment debtor, who was in the process of registering the vehicle in Lesotho at the time it was attached in execution.
[25]   I need to decide the two versions. In doing so, it is important to bear in mind the following test that is succinctly stated by Hennning J in
Embrahim v Deputy Sheriff Durban and Another:
[8]
“The test
whether a claimant has discharged the onus of proving his ownership to movable property which is not in his possession is whether, in a result, the probabilities are balanced in his favour. The strength of the evidence which he has to produce to succeed depends on the circumstances of the particular case.  In an interpleader suit, for example, the judgment creditor may be at a grave disadvantage because he is not in a position to adduce evidence to rebut that of a claimant who says that the disputed property is his, although he agreed to let the judgment debtor have possession of it.  Apart from other considerations, the Court would no doubt in such a case require, the claimant to produce clear and satisfactory proof of his ownership.  On the other hand, where the source of rebuttal evidence is available to and is utilised by the party who disputes the claimant’s claim, the position appears to be different; for then the disadvantage to which I have referred largely disappears.  I might mention one further factor which might be of particular importance in deciding whether the claimant’s evidence should be approached with more than normal caution, and that is the nature of the article of which the ownership is in dispute.”
[26]   Indisputably, the vehicle was found in possession of the first claimant, the judgment debtor, who was in the process of registering it in Lesotho. Nonetheless, the applicant has the certificate of registration for the vehicle in his names. At face value, the applicant may appear to have discharged the onus that was on him on the balance of probabilities. In my respectful view, he has not.
[27]   The applicant’s contention is that Lelingoana has not completed paying for the vehicle hence
“ownership of the vehicle has not yet been transferred to him as can be seen on the registration certificate
.” It is not his case that at the time the motor vehicle was imported or exported into Lesotho the parties had agreed that he will retain ownership thereof until full purchase price was paid. In my view, sole reliance on the registration certificate and that full purchase price has not yet been paid cannot help the applicant pass the threshold in the circumstances of this case.
[28]   The applicant’s founding affidavit is scant on details. It is threadbare and does not inspire confidence. The vehicle was attached and removed from the first claimant’s possession in November 2023. This is the time that the applicant says Lelingoana stopped making monthly payments for the vehicle. According to the applicant’s counsel, they got instruction from the applicant in July 2024. The applicant is as silent as a graveyard on why he instructed lawyers only in July 2024 and why they had to wait until September 2024 to approach the Registrar about interpleader proceedings.
[29]   In the last paragraph of his founding affidavit, the applicant acknowledges that
“it has been long now since the vehicle was attached and it has been packed (sic) where it is renting…
it being exposed to the harsh weather conditions and it is losing value in the process”.
Further, it is clear from paragraph 5.2 of his founding affidavit that the applicant is aware that after the vehicle was attached, the first claimant wanted it to be released to him claiming that it belongs to a certain company on whose behalf he was in the process of registering it when it was attached.
[30]   The applicant does not explain why he waited until February 2025 to institute the application. It is mindboggling that a legitimate owner of goods that are attached in execution can wait for more than a year to take a decisive action to claim his goods, more so when there is already a third party claiming them. In my respectful view, the applicant’s conduct is not consistent with that of legitimate owner of the vehicle.
[31]   I could not help but observed that the applicant is incredibly ambiguous on the nature of the transaction between him and Lelingoana, namely, whether it was sale for cash or sale on credit. The applicant asserts that some payment was made and that the parties agreed that the balance will be settled by monthly instalments. He does not tell this Court how much was paid and how much was the balance. Likewise, the period or date when the transaction was concluded is not disclosed. Neither is the paperwork in the form of an invoice or proof of payment annexed to the application.
[32]   The documents referred to in the preceding paragraph were going to reveal the identity of the purchaser.  Again, it is unimaginable that the applicant would have allowed the vehicle to leave South Africa without a written sale agreement if there was still outstanding balance on the purchase price. Further, this being a cross-border transaction, it is trite that it had to be subjected to customs and exercise as well as import value added tax regulatory regime.
[33]   In short, unless the vehicle was smuggled into the country, it is unavoidable to have paper trail regarding its importation and without which the vehicle was not lawfully going to be registered into Lesotho and be leased out at Polihali Dam construction. These customs declaration documents were going to shed light as to who the importer of the vehicle is. It is the applicant, as the seller, who was in a better position to adduce this evidence if it supports his case to dispel the contention that he is colluding with the judgment debtor.
[34]   Worse still the applicant has not joined the purported buyer into the proceedings, Lelingoana Lelingoana, or obtained a supporting affidavit from him to support his version regarding their agreement as well as the deposits he made. It bears stressing that it is the applicant who should have all the documents to corroborate his version that he sold the vehicle to Lelingoana Leligoana and not to the first claimant.
[35]   In warding off the suggestion that he is colluding with the first claimant who does not have prospects of success in his rescission application, the applicant denies knowledge of the rescission application in paragraph 2.3 of his Replying Affidavit. He goes further to assert that he is only aware of the application for release of the vehicle in which the first claimant says that it belongs to some company.
[36]   The applicant is utterly dishonest with this Court regarding his knowledge of the rescission application or lack thereof. He is the one who in the first place made mention of the rescission application and gave details thereof at paragraph 5.2 and 5.3 of his Founding Affidavit. This then begs a rhetorical question, why does the applicant plead ignorance of the rescission application when it is suggested that he is colluding with the first claimant. I have no reason not to believe that the fourth claimant has hit the nail on the head regarding the reason behind the “interpleader application.”
Disposition
[37]    A claimant (the applicant) must produce clear and satisfactory proof of ownership, especially in a case such as this one where circumstances invite suspicion. Considering all that has been said, the applicant has not discharged the onus to prove ownership of the vehicle attached. Registration certificate on its own is not conclusive proof of ownership. No additional documentation that could demonstrate that the applicant retained ownership of the vehicle was attached to the papers filed on his behalf.
[38]   For purposes of clarity, it is necessary to reiterate that it is the applicant who was in a better position to provide documentation to corroborate his version that he sold the vehicle to Lelingoana Lelingoana and not to the first claimant, the judgment debtor, as well as to demonstrate that he retained ownership.  This he dismally failed to do. His inaction after the vehicle was attached even as he was aware that the first claimant was claiming it speaks volumes.
Costs
[39]   There is no reason why costs cannot follow the event in this matter.
The Order
[40]   Therefore the application must fail, and the following order is made:
The application is dismissed with costs.
_________________________
A.R. MATHABA J
Judge of the High Court
For Applicant:                Adv. L Masoeu
For fourth claimant:         Adv. L Motsoehli

.

[3]

[4]

[2021] LSHC 38

which is erroneously referred to as section 7(2) in the judgment.
[5]
